content.text: ALZ-Bicep Accelerator

ALZ-Bicep is the official Microsoft-maintained open-source Bicep implementation of the Enterprise-Scale Landing Zone reference architecture. It deploys the full ESLZ platform — management group hierarchy, 500+ custom Azure Policy definitions, policy assignments across all management group tiers, hub networking, private DNS zones, and Log Analytics workspace — through four sequential Bicep modules. Each module is independently deployable and idempotent: re-running a module applies only the difference from the current state.

attrs.alt: ALZ-Bicep four-module structure and deployment flow

content.text: ALZ-Bicep is a starting point, not a locked template. The recommended workflow is to fork the repository, customize the parameter files for your tenant, and maintain your fork with upstream merges to receive Microsoft's policy updates. Do not modify the Bicep modules directly — parameter files are the customization surface.
ALZ-Bicep Module Breakdown
Module
Bicep file
Deploy scope
What it creates
Management Groups
managementGroupsAll.bicep
Tenant
alz root, alz-platform, alz-landing-zones, alz-corp, alz-online, alz-sandbox, alz-decommissioned, and sub-MGs
Policy Definitions
alzPolicyDefinitions.bicep
ALZ root MG
500+ custom policy definitions and 50+ initiative (policy set) definitions at the alz MG scope
Policy Assignments
alzPolicyAssignments.bicep
ALZ root MG (cascades)
Policy assignments at each MG tier (alz, alz-platform, alz-landing-zones, alz-corp, alz-online, alz-sandbox); creates system-assigned identities for remediation
Hub Networking
hubNetworking.bicep
Connectivity subscription
Hub VNet with all subnets, Azure Firewall Premium, Firewall Policy, ExpressRoute GW (optional), VPN GW (optional), Azure Bastion, all private DNS zones linked to hub VNet
Deploying ALZ-Bicep

The four modules are deployed in order. Module 2 (Policy Definitions) depends on Module 1 (Management Groups) because the definitions are scoped to the alz MG. Module 3 (Policy Assignments) depends on Module 2 because assignments reference the custom definition IDs. Module 4 (Hub Networking) is independent of the policy modules but requires the connectivity subscription to exist and be placed in the alz-platform-connectivity MG. Each deployment takes 5-15 minutes. The what-if command should be run before every deployment to preview changes.

# ALZ-Bicep deployment: four sequential modules
# Prerequisites: Owner on Tenant Root MG, az CLI with Bicep extension

# Clone the ALZ-Bicep repository
git clone https://github.com/Azure/ALZ-Bicep.git
cd ALZ-Bicep

# 1. Deploy management group hierarchy (tenant scope)
az deployment tenant create \
  --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/managementGroups/managementGroupsAll.bicep \
  --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/managementGroups/parameters/managementGroupsAll.parameters.all.json \
  --location eastus \
  --name 'alz-mg-$(date +%Y%m%d)'

# 2. Deploy custom policy definitions (MG scope at ALZ root)
az deployment mg create \
  --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/policy/definitions/alzPolicyDefinitions.bicep \
  --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/policy/definitions/parameters/alzPolicyDefinitions.parameters.all.json \
  --management-group-id alz \
  --location eastus \
  --name 'alz-policy-defs-$(date +%Y%m%d)'

# 3. Deploy policy assignments (MG scope — creates assignments on each MG)
az deployment mg create \
  --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/policy/assignments/alzPolicyAssignments.bicep \
  --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/policy/assignments/parameters/alzPolicyAssignments.parameters.prod.json \
  --management-group-id alz \
  --location eastus \
  --name 'alz-policy-assigns-$(date +%Y%m%d)'

# 4. Deploy hub networking (subscription scope in connectivity sub)
SUB_CONN='<connectivity-sub-id>'
az deployment sub create \
  --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/hubNetworking/hubNetworking.bicep \
  --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/hubNetworking/parameters/hubNetworking.parameters.prod.json \
  --subscription $SUB_CONN \
  --location eastus \
  --name 'alz-hub-network-$(date +%Y%m%d)'

echo 'ALZ-Bicep deployment complete.'
Parameter File Customization

ALZ-Bicep ships with default parameter files that match the reference architecture. Every organization-specific value (MG name prefix, hub address space, Firewall SKU, whether to deploy ExpressRoute or VPN gateway, which private DNS zones to create) is exposed as a parameter. Customize the parameter files in your fork rather than the Bicep modules — this keeps your fork mergeable with upstream changes.
What-If and Validation

The ARM what-if operation is mandatory before deploying ALZ-Bicep to production. It shows exactly which resources will be created, modified, or deleted without applying any changes. Policy assignment deployments are especially important to what-if because a new assignment can trigger immediate Deny effects on existing resources — knowing that a Deny policy will be assigned gives the team time to remediate non-compliant resources before the assignment blocks new deployments. Use --result-format ResourceIdOnly for policy modules (hundreds of resources) and FullResourcePayloads for networking modules (fewer resources, full diff useful).

# Run what-if before every ALZ-Bicep deployment to preview changes
# What-if shows creates, modifies, deletes, and no-changes without applying.

# What-if for management groups module
az deployment tenant what-if \
  --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/managementGroups/managementGroupsAll.bicep \
  --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/managementGroups/parameters/managementGroupsAll.parameters.all.json \
  --location eastus \
  --result-format FullResourcePayloads

# What-if for policy definitions
az deployment mg what-if \
  --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/policy/definitions/alzPolicyDefinitions.bicep \
  --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/policy/definitions/parameters/alzPolicyDefinitions.parameters.all.json \
  --management-group-id alz \
  --location eastus \
  --result-format ResourceIdOnly

# What-if for hub networking
az deployment sub what-if \
  --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/hubNetworking/hubNetworking.bicep \
  --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/hubNetworking/parameters/hubNetworking.parameters.prod.json \
  --subscription '<connectivity-sub-id>' \
  --location eastus \
  --exclude-change-types Ignore NoChange

# Validate Bicep compiles cleanly before deployment
az bicep build --file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/hubNetworking/hubNetworking.bicep
echo 'Bicep compilation: OK'
ALZ-Bicep vs Manual CLI
Consideration
ALZ-Bicep
Manual CLI / custom Bicep
Policy coverage
500+ Microsoft-maintained policy definitions; updated by Microsoft as new services launch
Write your own — good for targeted policies; misses breadth of ESLZ set
Time to deploy
4 modules, ~30 minutes total for a full ESLZ
Weeks to write equivalent IaC from scratch
Maintenance
Fork and merge upstream; Microsoft releases new ALZ-Bicep versions regularly
Fully self-maintained; policy updates require manual work
Customization
Parameter files only (no Bicep changes required for most scenarios)
Full flexibility — any resource, any configuration
Risk
Well-tested by Microsoft and the community; production-proven at scale
Custom code carries higher implementation risk; needs thorough testing
When to choose
Default for new ESLZ deployments; best for teams without existing Azure IaC
When you have >50% of ESLZ already deployed or have specific constraints the accelerator doesn't support
GitHub Actions Pipeline for ALZ-Bicep

The recommended way to deploy ALZ-Bicep is via a CI/CD pipeline that runs what-if on every pull request and deploys on merge to main. GitHub Actions with OIDC (Workload Identity Federation) authentication is the Microsoft-recommended approach — no client secrets stored in GitHub, just the client ID and tenant ID. The deployment environment approval gate in GitHub prevents accidental production deployments without a human review step.

# GitHub Actions workflow for ALZ-Bicep deployment
# Runs what-if on PRs, deploys on merge to main.

# .github/workflows/alz-deploy.yml
cat << 'EOF'
name: ALZ-Bicep Deployment

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - 'infra-as-code/bicep/**'
      - '.github/workflows/alz-deploy.yml'
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - 'infra-as-code/bicep/**'

permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read

jobs:
  validate:
    name: Validate Bicep
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
          tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}
      - name: Build Bicep (compile check)
        run: |
          az bicep install
          find infra-as-code/bicep/modules -name '*.bicep' -exec az bicep build --file {} \;
      - name: What-if management groups
        run: |
          az deployment tenant what-if \
            --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/managementGroups/managementGroupsAll.bicep \
            --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/managementGroups/parameters/managementGroupsAll.parameters.all.json \
            --location eastus --result-format ResourceIdOnly

  deploy:
    name: Deploy ALZ-Bicep
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: validate
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push'
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
          tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}
      - name: Deploy Module 1 - Management Groups
        run: |
          az deployment tenant create \
            --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/managementGroups/managementGroupsAll.bicep \
            --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/managementGroups/parameters/managementGroupsAll.parameters.all.json \
            --location eastus --name 'alz-mg-${{ github.run_number }}'
      - name: Deploy Module 2 - Policy Definitions
        run: |
          az deployment mg create \
            --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/policy/definitions/alzPolicyDefinitions.bicep \
            --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/policy/definitions/parameters/alzPolicyDefinitions.parameters.all.json \
            --management-group-id alz --location eastus \
            --name 'alz-policy-defs-${{ github.run_number }}'
      - name: Deploy Module 3 - Policy Assignments
        run: |
          az deployment mg create \
            --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/policy/assignments/alzPolicyAssignments.bicep \
            --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/policy/assignments/parameters/alzPolicyAssignments.parameters.prod.json \
            --management-group-id alz --location eastus \
            --name 'alz-policy-assigns-${{ github.run_number }}'
      - name: Deploy Module 4 - Hub Networking
        run: |
          az deployment sub create \
            --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/hubNetworking/hubNetworking.bicep \
            --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/hubNetworking/parameters/hubNetworking.parameters.prod.json \
            --subscription '${{ secrets.CONNECTIVITY_SUB_ID }}' \
            --location eastus \
            --name 'alz-hub-net-${{ github.run_number }}'
EOF
Keeping Your Fork Up to Date

Microsoft releases ALZ-Bicep updates monthly. Updates include new policy definitions for newly launched Azure services, bug fixes to existing policies, Bicep syntax improvements, and new optional modules. The update workflow is: fetch upstream, review the CHANGELOG, merge upstream/main into a feature branch, resolve conflicts in parameter files (Bicep module files should be accepted as-is from upstream), run what-if against dev, and merge via PR. Parameter files are the only conflict-prone files because they contain your tenant-specific values.

# Keep your ALZ-Bicep fork up to date with upstream releases
# ALZ-Bicep releases new versions monthly with policy updates and new features.

# 1. Add upstream remote (one-time setup)
git remote add upstream https://github.com/Azure/ALZ-Bicep.git
git fetch upstream

# 2. Check what's changed in the upstream since your last merge
LAST_MERGE=$(git log --oneline --merges | grep 'upstream' | head -1 | awk '{print $1}')
git log --oneline $LAST_MERGE..upstream/main -- 'infra-as-code/bicep/modules/'

# 3. Review the upstream CHANGELOG
git show upstream/main:CHANGELOG.md | head -100

# 4. Merge upstream into a feature branch
git checkout -b merge/alz-bicep-upstream-$(date +%Y%m)
git merge upstream/main --no-commit --no-ff

# 5. Resolve conflicts (parameter files are the most common conflict location)
# Your customizations live in *parameters*.json files — those are the conflict sites.
# Accept upstream changes to *.bicep files; merge your values into parameter files.
git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U

# 6. After resolving conflicts, run what-if against dev/staging
az deployment tenant what-if \
  --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/managementGroups/managementGroupsAll.bicep \
  --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/managementGroups/parameters/managementGroupsAll.parameters.all.json \
  --location eastus

# 7. Commit, push, open PR for review before merging to main
git add -A && git commit -m "merge: ALZ-Bicep upstream $(git describe --tags upstream/main)"
git push -u origin merge/alz-bicep-upstream-$(date +%Y%m)
Running Policy Remediations After Deployment

ALZ-Bicep's Module 3 deploys policy assignments but does not retroactively apply DeployIfNotExists or Modify effects to existing resources. Remediation tasks bridge this gap. After every ALZ-Bicep deployment that includes new or updated policy assignments, create remediation tasks for each assignment that has a deployable effect. The tasks run asynchronously; check their status with az policy remediation list. In a greenfield ESLZ (no existing workloads), remediations are less critical since all resources created after assignment inherit the effect automatically.

# Run policy remediations after ALZ-Bicep policy assignments are deployed
# Policy assignments with DeployIfNotExists or Modify effects do NOT retroactively
# apply to existing resources — remediation tasks handle the backfill.

ALZ_SCOPE='/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/alz'

# List all policy assignments with remediation-capable effects
az policy assignment list \
  --scope $ALZ_SCOPE \
  --query '[].{Name:name, DisplayName:displayName}' -o table

# Create remediation tasks for the key assignments
# 1. Diagnostic settings for Activity Log
az policy remediation create \
  --name 'remediate-activity-diag' \
  --policy-assignment 'Deploy-azActivity-Log' \
  --resource-discovery-mode ExistingNonCompliant \
  --scope $ALZ_SCOPE

# 2. Enable Defender for Cloud CSPM
az policy remediation create \
  --name 'remediate-mdfc-cspm' \
  --policy-assignment 'Deploy-MDFC-Config' \
  --resource-discovery-mode ExistingNonCompliant \
  --scope $ALZ_SCOPE

# 3. Deploy Log Analytics agent (for subscriptions with existing VMs)
az policy remediation create \
  --name 'remediate-log-agent' \
  --policy-assignment 'Deploy-Log-Analytics' \
  --resource-discovery-mode ExistingNonCompliant \
  --scope $ALZ_SCOPE

# Check remediation status
az policy remediation list \
  --scope $ALZ_SCOPE \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Status:provisioningState, Resources:resourceCount}' \
  -o table

# Monitor compliance score improvement after remediation
az security regulatory-compliance-standards list \
  --subscription '<management-sub-id>' \
  --query '[].{Standard:name, Passed:passedControls, Failed:failedControls}' \
  -o table
Common ALZ-Bicep Mistakes
Mistake
What breaks
Fix
Deploying modules out of order (4 before 2)
Policy assignments reference custom definition IDs from Module 2 that do not exist yet
Always deploy in order: 1 (MG) → 2 (policy defs) → 3 (assignments) → 4 (hub network)
Modifying Bicep module files directly
Upstream merges produce conflicts in every modified file; upgrade path breaks
Customization belongs in parameter files only; Bicep modules are upstream-maintained
Not running what-if before policy assignment deployment
New Deny-effect assignments block resource creation in existing subscriptions immediately
Run what-if before every deployment; fix non-compliant resources before assigning Deny policies
Forgetting to run remediation tasks after Module 3
Existing resources violate the new policies but are never brought into compliance
After every Module 3 deployment, create remediation tasks for all DeployIfNotExists/Modify assignments
Using client secrets for GitHub Actions authentication
Secrets expire and rotate; if not rotated, deployments fail silently
Use OIDC / Workload Identity Federation — no secrets to rotate
Deploying ALZ-Bicep without placing platform subscriptions first
Module 4 (hub networking) targets the connectivity subscription which must already be in alz-platform-connectivity MG
Create and place the three platform subscriptions before running Module 4
Summary

ALZ-Bicep is the fastest path to a production-ready ESLZ. It deploys four sequential modules: Management Groups (tenant scope), Policy Definitions (ALZ root MG), Policy Assignments (cascading across all MG tiers), and Hub Networking (connectivity subscription). Customization happens exclusively through parameter files — fork the repository, edit parameter files, and run what-if before every deployment. The policy module installs 500+ Microsoft-maintained definitions that cover the eight ESLZ design areas and are updated as new Azure services launch.
